behaviors.description: SUM(A1:A10) evaluates to the correct sum of the values in cells A1 through A10.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure lives entirely inside the range-parser and summation logic of a single evaluator module. No module boundary crossing — a constructed cell grid (in-memory array or map) is all the test needs. REAL-INFRA: No OS, no network. LLM-DEP: None. MULTI-STEP: None. REJECTION-GATE: Integration rejected — the failure mode (wrong sum or off-by-one range) does not require multiple modules to be wired; a unit test with a stubbed cell store exposes it fully.
behaviors.test_description: Construct an in-memory cell map with A1=1, A2=2, …, A10=10. Invoke the formula evaluator directly with '=SUM(A1:A10)'. Assert the result equals 55. Also test boundary cases: all-zero cells (→0), single-cell range, and cells containing floats.

behaviors.description: A cell referencing another cell that contains a formula (e.g., =B1+1 where B1 =A1*2) produces the correct cascaded result.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure only surfaces when the cell-lookup layer, the dependency resolver, and the evaluator are all wired together — a bug in any handoff (e.g., evaluator using stale B1 value instead of re-evaluating it) is invisible if B1 is stubbed as a constant. REAL-INFRA: No OS/network needed. LLM-DEP: None. MULTI-STEP: None. REJECTION-GATE: Unit rejected — stubbing B1 as a pre-computed constant bypasses the exact failure mode (cascaded formula resolution).
behaviors.test_description: Wire together the cell store, dependency resolver, and formula evaluator. Set A1=3 as a raw value, B1='=A1*2', and C1='=B1+1'. Trigger full evaluation. Assert C1==7. Also assert that changing A1 to 5 and re-evaluating yields C1==11, proving cascaded re-evaluation, not just first-pass correctness.

behaviors.description: The engine detects a circular dependency (A1 references B1, B1 references A1) and returns a circular reference error instead of looping.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure (infinite recursion or missing cycle check) is in the cycle-detection algorithm itself — a graph with two nodes pointing at each other fully exposes it. The dependency graph and the error-return path live in a single logical unit. REAL-INFRA: None. LLM-DEP: None. MULTI-STEP: None. REJECTION-GATE: Integration rejected — the cycle detection does not require multiple modules to be wired; a constructed dependency-graph object passed to the detection function is sufficient to trigger and observe the failure.
behaviors.test_description: Construct a dependency graph where node A1 depends on B1 and B1 depends on A1. Invoke the cycle-detection routine (or the evaluator's entry point) on A1. Assert the return value is a circular-reference error sentinel, not a numeric result. Also test a three-node cycle (A→B→C→A) and a non-cycle graph to confirm no false positives.

behaviors.description: VLOOKUP with an exact match returns the correct value from the lookup table, and returns an error when the key is not found.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is inside the VLOOKUP function implementation — key comparison, column-index extraction, and not-found signaling. All inputs (lookup key, table data, column index) can be passed as in-memory structures. REAL-INFRA: None. LLM-DEP: None. MULTI-STEP: None. REJECTION-GATE: Integration rejected — the behavior text is about the function's own logic, not about how it wires to cell storage or a persistence layer.
behaviors.test_description: Build an in-memory 4-row × 3-column lookup table. Invoke VLOOKUP with a key present in column 1; assert the correct column-2 value is returned. Invoke VLOOKUP with a missing key; assert an #N/A or equivalent error is returned. Also test: first-row match, last-row match, and type mismatch (numeric key vs string data).

behaviors.description: The IF function correctly handles nested conditions (e.g., =IF(A1>10, IF(A1>20, 'high', 'medium'), 'low')).
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is in the expression-parser's recursive descent through nested IF arms and in the evaluator's branch selection. All three code paths (low/medium/high) are exercisable with a single cell containing a known numeric value — no other module needed. REAL-INFRA: None. LLM-DEP: None. MULTI-STEP: None. REJECTION-GATE: Integration rejected — stubbing A1 as a constant integer exposes all three branches without wiring to a cell store.
behaviors.test_description: For the formula '=IF(A1>10, IF(A1>20, "high", "medium"), "low")', evaluate with A1=5 (expect 'low'), A1=15 (expect 'medium'), A1=25 (expect 'high'), and A1=10 (boundary, expect 'low'). Assert exact string results. Also test with A1=20 (boundary of inner condition, expect 'medium').

behaviors.description: CONCATENATE joins cell values with the correct separator and handles empty cells by skipping them rather than inserting blank strings.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is in CONCATENATE's own logic — specifically the 'skip empty vs insert blank' branch and separator insertion. Cell values can be passed as literals without a cell store. REAL-INFRA: None. LLM-DEP: None. MULTI-STEP: None. REJECTION-GATE: Integration rejected — 'empty cell skipping' is a logic branch within the function, not a property of how the function interacts with a cell storage layer.
behaviors.test_description: Call CONCATENATE with ['Hello', '', 'World'] and separator ', '. Assert result is 'Hello, World' (empty string skipped, not 'Hello, , World'). Also test: all-empty inputs (→ empty string), single non-empty cell, no separator (default), and cells containing numeric values (type coercion check).

behaviors.description: A formula referencing a cell in a different sheet (e.g., =Sheet2!A1+1) resolves correctly when the referenced sheet exists.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure appears at the boundary between the sheet registry (which must locate Sheet2) and the formula evaluator (which must resolve the cross-sheet reference). If Sheet2 is stubbed as a constant, the registry-lookup path is never exercised and a bug there is invisible. REAL-INFRA: No OS needed — an in-process sheet registry with real Sheet2 object is sufficient. LLM-DEP: None. MULTI-STEP: None. REJECTION-GATE: Unit rejected — the cross-sheet lookup requires the registry and evaluator to interact; a constant stub bypasses the exact failure mode.
behaviors.test_description: Create a workbook with two in-memory sheets: Sheet1 (empty formula cell) and Sheet2 (A1=42). Evaluate '=Sheet2!A1+1' in Sheet1!A1. Assert result is 43. Also test: referenced sheet does not exist (expect #REF! error); referenced cell is itself a formula on Sheet2 (cascaded cross-sheet); sheet name contains spaces or special characters.

behaviors.description: When a referenced cell's value changes, all dependent formulas are recalculated in the correct topological order.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure (wrong recalc order, missing dependents) only appears when the change-propagation system, dependency graph, topological sort, and evaluator are all wired together and a value is mutated. REAL-INFRA: No OS needed. LLM-DEP: None. MULTI-STEP: None. REJECTION-GATE: Unit rejected — topological sort alone does not prove that the engine correctly re-evaluates in that order when a real cell mutation triggers propagation.
behaviors.test_description: Wire up a diamond dependency: A1=raw, B1='=A1*2', C1='=A1+1', D1='=B1+C1'. Set A1=3, evaluate all cells, assert D1==10. Mutate A1 to 5, trigger propagation, assert D1==16. Instrument or observe evaluation order to confirm B1 and C1 are both recalculated before D1. Also test a deeper chain to catch early-stop bugs.

behaviors.description: The undo operation after editing a formula restores both the formula text and all previously computed dependent values.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure (dependent cells showing stale or recomputed-wrong values after undo) only appears when the undo stack, cell store, and evaluator are wired together — the undo must restore formula text AND trigger a full re-evaluation of dependents. REAL-INFRA: No OS needed. LLM-DEP: None. MULTI-STEP: None. REJECTION-GATE: Unit rejected — testing the undo stack in isolation cannot verify that dependent values are correctly restored because that requires the evaluator to re-run against the restored state.
behaviors.test_description: Set A1=5, B1='=A1*2' (B1==10). Edit A1's value to 99 (B1 recalculates to 198). Invoke undo. Assert A1==5, B1==10, and the formula text in B1 is unchanged. Also test: undo after adding a new formula (not just editing a value); multiple consecutive undos; undo with a three-cell dependency chain.

behaviors.description: Saving a workbook to disk and loading it back produces identical cell values and formulas — no data loss or precision change in floating-point results.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure (precision loss, formula truncation, type coercion) is in the serialization/deserialization logic itself — it is fully exercisable with an in-memory byte buffer (BytesIO). REAL-INFRA: The behavior says 'to disk' but the precision and formula-preservation failure modes do not require actual OS filesystem events; a BytesIO round-trip exposes the same bugs. REJECTION-GATE: System considered because 'to disk' is stated. Disqualifying text: the failure modes described ('no data loss or precision change') are purely about the binary format encoding, not about OS-level file semantics (permissions, buffering, concurrent writes) — an in-process round-trip suffices. LLM-DEP: None. MULTI-STEP: None.
behaviors.test_description: Build a workbook with: an integer cell (42), a float cell (3.141592653589793), a formula cell ('=A1+A2'), and a cross-sheet reference. Serialize to a BytesIO buffer via the save routine, then deserialize via the load routine. Assert all cell values match bit-for-bit (float equality with no rounding), all formula strings are preserved exactly, and the cross-sheet reference resolves to the same value after load.
